title: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
one_line_summary: This review synthesizes evidence on the epidemiology, phenotypes, and risk factors of neurological complications in **critically** ill patients without primary neurological disease, highlighting modifiable contributors and knowledge gaps.
key_pearls: Neurological complications are common and underrecognized in **critically** ill patients without primary brain injury, with autopsy studies revealing a far greater burden of occult injury than is detected clinically.

Delirium occurs in approximately **one-third** of ICU patients and is an independent predictor of increased **mortality**, longer mechanical ventilation, and long-term cognitive impairment comparable to moderate traumatic brain injury.

More than **90%** of seizures in **critically** ill patients are non-convulsive, necessitating a high index of suspicion and continuous EEG for detection, as reliance on clinical examination alone underestimates seizure burden.
ICU-acquired weakness (ICUAW) has an incidence exceeding **40%** and is associated with prolonged mechanical ventilation, increased 5-year **mortality**, and persistent functional impairment.
Benzodiazepine sedatives are associated with a higher delirium incidence compared to alternatives like *dexmedetomidine*, which may reduce delirium prevalence.

The **ABCDEF bundle** (Assess/manage pain, spontaneous awakening/breathing trials, Choice of sedation, Delirium monitoring, Early mobility, Family engagement) is associated with reduced delirium burden, shorter lengths of stay, and lower **mortality**.

Long-term cognitive impairment affects **30-50%** of ICU survivors at 6-12 months, with deficits in memory, attention, and executive function, and is strongly associated with longer duration of delirium.
population: Adult ICU patients with systemic critical illness and without a primary neurological diagnosis at admission

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
- Neurological complications contribute substantially to morbidity, **mortality**, and long-term disability in ICU patients admitted for non-neurological conditions.
- The burden encompasses incidence, prevalence, and downstream clinical impact including prolonged ICU stay, increased healthcare utilization, and long-term functional and cognitive impairment.
- Delirium, stroke, seizures, and neuromuscular disorders are common yet frequently underrecognized manifestations.
- The brain is particularly vulnerable to physiological stresses of **critical** illness: **hypoxemia**, **hypotension**, **inflammation**, **metabolic derangements**, and iatrogenic factors like mechanical ventilation and sedation.
- This review aims to integrate contemporary evidence on the overall burden, shared and distinct risk factors, and clinical management considerations.

sections.heading: Approach to Evidence Synthesis
sections.content:
- A structured search of PubMed, Embase, and the Cochrane Library was conducted for studies from January 2000 through March 2026.
- Search terms included combinations of “**critical** illness,” “intensive care,” “neurological complications,” “acute brain injury,” “encephalopathy,” “delirium,” “stroke,” “seizures,” “ICU acquired weakness,” “psychological impairment,” and “cognitive impairment.”
- Seminal studies published prior to the search window were included when foundational.
- Focus was on adult ICU populations with systemic **critical** illness without a primary neurological diagnosis.
- Randomized trials, large observational cohorts, meta-analyses, and contemporary consensus statements were prioritized.
- Findings were synthesized qualitatively due to expected heterogeneity in study design, populations, and outcome definitions.

sections.heading: Epidemiology of Neurological Complications of Critical Illness
sections.content:
- Acute brain injury is both common and underrecognized in **critically** ill patients without a primary neurological diagnosis.
- A meta-analysis of clinical studies reported ischemic stroke in only **5%** of cases, while autopsy studies showed histological evidence of acute brain injury in nearly half of patients.
- Ischemic stroke was the most frequent lesion in autopsy studies (**45%**), followed by hemorrhagic stroke (**26%**).
- Delirium is one of the most common manifestations, occurring in approximately **one-third** of ICU patients overall, and exceeding **50%** in mechanically ventilated or higher-severity cohorts.
- Delirium is independently associated with increased **mortality**, longer duration of mechanical ventilation, and extended ICU and hospital length of stay.
- Other common complications include seizures, **critical** illness polyneuropathy and myopathy, particularly among patients with sepsis or prolonged ICU stays.
- Persistent cognitive impairment affects approximately **one-third to one-half** of ICU survivors at ~6–12 months after discharge.
- Deficits commonly involve memory, attention, and executive function, comparable in severity to moderate traumatic brain injury or mild Alzheimer’s disease.
- Many patients also experience persistent psychological and neuromuscular impairments contributing to long-term disability.
section_pearls: Neuropathological data suggest the true prevalence of brain injury is considerably higher than detected clinically, with a striking gap between clinically recognized cerebrovascular events and postmortem evidence.

sections.heading: Mechanisms of Secondary Brain Injury
sections.content:
- Systemic physiological disturbances compromise cerebral oxygen delivery, perfusion, and neurocellular homeostasis.
- **Hypoxemia**, **anemia**, and impaired oxygen utilization reduce cerebral oxygen delivery and increase vulnerability to regional brain hypoxia.
- Hemodynamic instability, most notably **hypotension**, may further compromise cerebral perfusion, exacerbated by **critical** illness-associated impairment of cerebral autoregulation.
- Sepsis and systemic inflammation can directly injure the brain through endothelial activation, microcirculatory dysfunction, blood–brain barrier disruption, and neuroinflammatory signaling.
- Additional contributors include abnormalities in arterial carbon dioxide tension, metabolic disturbances (dysglycemia, electrolyte derangements, hepatic or renal failure), and iatrogenic exposures (sedative and analgesic medications).
- Neuroimaging findings in survivors include global and regional cerebral atrophy, white matter disruption, cortical and subcortical lesions, and focal ischemic or hemorrhagic lesions.
- Susceptibility-weighted MRI has demonstrated cerebral microbleeds, often involving the corpus callosum and juxtacortical white matter, implicating microvascular injury.

sections.heading: Specific Neurological Complications
sections.content:
- Acute encephalopathy reflects a rapidly evolving global disturbance of cerebral function manifesting as changes in cognition, level of consciousness, and behavior.
- An international consensus framework defines acute encephalopathy as a rapidly developing (typically within < **4 weeks**) pathobiological brain process expressed clinically as subsyndromal delirium, delirium, or coma.
- Delirium and coma are clinical expressions along a spectrum of disordered consciousness arising from this shared substrate.
- Sepsis-associated encephalopathy reflects attribution of this shared substrate to sepsis as the precipitating etiology.
- **ICU Delirium**:
  - Formally defined by an abnormal, fluctuating course of mental status, inattention, and disorganized thinking.
  - The hypoactive subtype accounts for **40–60%** of delirium presentations and is the easiest to miss.
  - Delirium is an independent predictor of higher **mortality**, longer duration of mechanical ventilation, prolonged hospital length of stay, increased healthcare costs, and higher rates of long-term cognitive impairment.
  - Predisposing risk factors include advanced age, pre-existing cognitive impairment, frailty, and chronic alcohol use.
  - Precipitating factors include severity of illness, respiratory failure, shock, sedative medications, and environmental factors.
  - The **ABCDEF bundle** has been associated with reduced delirium burden, shorter lengths of stay, and lower **mortality**.
- **Seizures**:
  - In non-neurological ICUs, **8–15%** of patients experience seizures.
  - More than **90%** of seizures in **critically** ill patients are non-convulsive.
  - Diagnosis requires a high degree of suspicion and EEG; continuous EEG has higher sensitivity than intermittent EEG.
  - Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, cessation of seizures with antiseizure medications, and supportive care.
- **Strokes**:
  - Most commonly present with new hemiplegia, delirium, and seizures.
  - Diagnosis is frequently delayed and clinically challenging due to sedation, neuromuscular blockade, and pre-existing encephalopathy.
  - Head and neck CT with CT angiography (CT/CTA) remains the primary diagnostic modality.
  - Major sources for ischemic strokes include cardiac interventions, cardiovascular devices (LVADs, ECMO), atrial fibrillation, endocarditis, and sepsis-associated coagulopathy.
  - Management must be individualized; many ICU patients have contraindications to systemic thrombolysis.
  - Hemorrhagic strokes in the ICU more frequently occur in the context of coagulopathy secondary to underlying medical comorbidities.
- **ICU-Acquired Neuromuscular Disorders**:
  - ICU-acquired weakness (ICUAW) is a newly developed, generalized, symmetrical weakness affecting limb and respiratory muscles.
  - Reported incidence is more than **40%** among patients recovering slowly from **critical** illness.
  - Major risk factors include sepsis, severity of illness, prolonged mechanical ventilation, immobility, hyperglycemia, and exposure to corticosteroids or neuromuscular blocking agents.
  - ICUAW is associated with prolonged mechanical ventilation, extended ICU and hospital length of stay, increased 1-year and 5-year **mortality**, and persistent functional impairment.
  - Diagnosis is typically based on the Medical Research Council sum score (MRCss), with values < **48** (out of 60) indicating clinically **significant** weakness.
  - Handgrip strength below **11 kg** for males and **7 kg** for females suggests ICUAW.
  - Management remains largely supportive, focusing on early mobilization, minimization of deep sedation, and optimization of metabolic and inflammatory factors.
- **Long-Term Neuropsychological Sequelae**:
  - At 1 year, cognitive impairment remained common among ARDS survivors: **30%** showed global decline, and **78%** had at least one domain affected.
  - The BRAIN-ICU study reported prevalence of cognitive impairment at 1 year comparable to moderate traumatic brain injury (**34%**) and mild Alzheimer-type dementia (**24%**).
  - Long-term cognitive impairment was strongly associated with longer duration of delirium but not independently associated with sedative or analgesic exposure.
  - PTSD prevalence was reported at approximately **28%** at 2–10 years after ICU discharge among ARDS survivors.
section_pearls: Acute encephalopathy and delirium are often used interchangeably but represent a pathobiological substrate and its clinical expression, respectively.
The hypoactive subtype of delirium accounts for 40-60% of presentations and is the easiest to miss without structured screening.
Continuous EEG is critical for detecting non-convulsive seizures, which constitute over 90% of seizures in the ICU.

sections.heading: Specific Risk Factors for Neurological Complications
sections.content:
- **Patient-Related Risk Factors**:
  - Pre-existing vulnerabilities: advanced age, chronic alcohol use, history of neurological or psychiatric disorders (prior stroke, epilepsy, cognitive impairment).
  - Frailty is a risk factor for sepsis-associated delirium.
  - Nutritional deficiencies (e.g., thiamine deficiency leading to Wernicke’s encephalopathy) may further increase risk.
  - **Critical** illness-related factors: underlying cause and severity of illness, organ dysfunction (respiratory failure, shock, renal or hepatic failure).
  - Sepsis is a major risk factor for ICUAW.
- **Treatment-Related Risk Factors: Medications**:
  - *Benzodiazepines* (e.g., midazolam) are associated with a higher delirium incidence and longer delirium duration.
  - *Dexmedetomidine* may reduce delirium prevalence and is a preferred sedative agent.
  - *Cefepime* has been shown to precipitate toxic-metabolic encephalopathy, with neurotoxicity occurring in up to **15%** of ICU patients receiving the drug.
  - *Carbapenems* (e.g., imipenem) can provoke seizures via GABA inhibition, especially with renal impairment.
  - *Fluoroquinolones* can cause agitation, insomnia, delirium, or seizures.
  - *Linezolid* can lead to serotonin syndrome, especially when combined with other serotonergic medications.
  - *Calcineurin inhibitors* (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) are frequently implicated in drug-induced encephalopathies and can cause posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES).
- **Treatment-Related Risk Factors: Advanced Physiologic Support**:
  - *Mechanical Ventilation*: Positive pressure ventilation may reduce venous return and raise intracranial pressure (ICP). Hyperventilation-induced hypocapnia causes cerebral vasoconstriction and reduced cerebral blood flow.
  - *ECMO*: Veno-venous ECMO is associated with neurologic complications in **7%** of cases, including intracerebral hemorrhage, ischemic stroke, and seizures. A large relative decrease in PaCO2 in the first 24 hours is independently associated with increased neurological complications.
  - *Renal Replacement Therapy*: Neurologic complications arise from underlying kidney failure, treatment-related physiological shifts, and procedural factors. Dialysis disequilibrium syndrome (DDS) is a classical but now uncommon complication. CRRT may mitigate rapid osmotic shifts and is often preferred in patients with acute brain injury.
section_pearls: Cefepime-induced neurotoxicity may occur in up to 15% of ICU patients, with a tenfold higher risk of delirium/neurotoxicity compared to meropenem.
A large relative decrease in PaCO2 in the first 24 hours after ECMO initiation is independently associated with an increased incidence of neurological complications.

sections.heading: Prevention and Treatment: A Multicomponent Approach
sections.content:
- Prevention of neurological complications depends on coordinated, multicomponent strategies rather than single interventions.
- Sedation practices affect neuroinflammatory signaling, sleep–wake regulation, delirium burden, and metabolic function.
- Early and structured mobility programs may preserve muscle function and reduce ICU-acquired weakness, with broader systemic and neurocognitive effects.
- Coordinated implementation of non-pharmacologic bundles, most notably the **ABCDEF ICU Liberation framework**, has been associated with greater benefit than isolated interventions alone.
- In selected high-risk patients, continuous EEG may improve detection of non-convulsive status epilepticus and other occult epileptiform activity.
section_pearls: The ABCDEF bundle is a cornerstone of prevention, with coordinated implementation showing greater benefit than isolated interventions.

sections.heading: Future Areas of Research
sections.content:
- **Significant** knowledge gaps remain in the understanding of neurological complications of non-neurological **critical** illness.
- Large, prospective cohort studies integrating multimodal neuromonitoring, neuroimaging, and molecular biomarkers are needed.
- Biomarker-based approaches may refine phenotyping of acute encephalopathy and delirium by distinguishing underlying biological mechanisms.
- Artificial intelligence and digital phenotyping could enable continuous, individualized detection of encephalopathy, seizures, and evolving delirium trajectories.
- Interventional trials should test pragmatic, brain-directed care bundles that combine sedation minimization, early mobilization, and physiologic optimization.
- Harmonized outcome measures and multicenter data platforms are **essential** for tracking survivorship trajectories.

sections.heading: Conclusion
sections.content:
- Patients remain vulnerable to acute and long-term neurological dysfunction related to **critical** illness and life-sustaining interventions.
- ICU-acquired brain injury is frequently associated with persistent cognitive and neuropsychiatric impairment, even among patients without primary neurologic diagnoses.
- Discrepancies between systematic surveillance and routine clinical recognition suggest that the reported incidence of neurological complications may underestimate their true burden.
- Preserving brain health in the ICU is **essential** not only for **survival** but for optimizing long-term cognitive and functional recovery.

strengths_limitations: Strengths: - Integrates contemporary evidence on the overall burden, shared and distinct risk factors, and clinical management considerations of neurological complications in a unified framework. - Informed by a structured search of major databases and prioritizes randomized trials, large observational cohorts, and consensus statements. Limitations: - Heterogeneity in case definitions, diagnostic criteria, and surveillance practices across studies limits comparability and may obscure the true scope of neurological injury. - Findings were synthesized qualitatively due to expected heterogeneity, without formal GRADE methodology or pooled estimates. - Many associations are derived from observational studies and may be confounded by illness severity and indication.

drugs_doses.indication: Sedation in mechanically ventilated ICU patients
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Higher delirium incidence and longer delirium duration compared with alternative agents; GABA-ergic excess, impaired arousal pathways, altered sleep architecture.

drugs_doses.indication: Sedation in ICU patients, preferred agent to reduce delirium
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: May cause hypotension and bradycardia; lacks significant GABA or anticholinergic activity.

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; dose adjustment required in renal failure
drugs_doses.indication: Treatment of infections in ICU patients
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Toxic-metabolic encephalopathy characterized by confusion, altered EEG with generalized periodic discharges, non-convulsive seizures; neurotoxicity in up to 15% of ICU patients; GABA-A receptor antagonism.

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; dose adjustment required in renal impairment
drugs_doses.indication: Treatment of infections in ICU patients
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Provokes seizures via GABA inhibition, especially with renal impairment.

drugs_doses.indication: Treatment of infections in ICU patients
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Agitation, insomnia, delirium, seizures; possible NMDA receptor overactivation.

drugs_doses.indication: Treatment of infections in ICU patients
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Serotonin syndrome (confusion, neuromuscular abnormalities, seizures), especially when combined with other serotonergic medications.
Drug-induced encephalopathies, headache, tremors, visual changes, mental status alteration, seizures, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES).
ICU-acquired weakness, prolonged neuromuscular junction dysfunction, muscle atrophy; higher risk with prolonged exposure and concurrent corticosteroids.

drugs_doses.indication: Anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive therapy in ICU
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: ICU-acquired weakness, muscle catabolism, neuropsychiatric symptoms; risk increased with prolonged exposure and co-exposures.
